## Summary of Analysis
We analyzed this idea:
"The course should have time dedicated to getting help because it would be beneficial for students who may be struggling AND have time conflicts during office hours/tutoring times."

This idea is more valuable than the others brainstormed, as this idea will allow us to delve into the class's perspective on the effectiveness of office hours and tutoring sessions. Through this, if there is an aggregate positive consensus about the effectiveness of office hours and tutoring sessions, we argue that there would be benefits in incorporating these out-of-class resources into class hours as well. We chose this idea over the others as we believe that there are existing survey questions that would shed light on the effectiveness of office hours and tutoring sessions. Furthermore, there are no existing questions from the survey that look into the other ideas that we posited.

We made a pie chart showcasing the distribution of a Comp 110 class (Freshman - Senior). Then we looked into the frequency of office hour visits. Then, we broke each grade level and analyzed the frequency of office hour visits per class to see if there was a discrepancy between office hour visits amongst the grades. This could showcase if some grades are busier/have conflicting hours with COMP 110 office hours.

## Conclusion

Overall, there was a similar distribution of office hour attendance amongst the 4 grades. Through this, we can conclude that grade levels do not influence attendance at office hours. It may be the case that there is a similar number of students who do not need help with the course. Resultantly, these students won't feel the need to attend office hours. This refutes our initial hypothesis, where we believed that grade level may have played a part in office hours attendance, as we believed that seniors/juniors (upperclassmen) would have more conflicting schedules in comparison to underclassmen. 

One variable we could have incorporated into our data was an individual's prior experience with coding. If an individual felt confident in their coding abilities, it would be unlikely for them to go to office hours. Despite this, using multiple variables to come to a conclusion may require multivariate statistical analysis methods--which are methods we haven't been exposed to yet. 

Honestly, I think that the current office hour schedule is very flexible, and the issues don't arise from the COMP 110 team; rather, it's the students' decisions that influence whether or not they want to go to office hours (conflicting schedules are very unlikely with the amount of office hour opportunities present through the week!). Consequently, we argue that there would be no stakeholders losing/benefitting as we aren't suggesting any changes to the existing structure of COMP 110.

Since the sample size for freshmen through seniors is greater than 30, we can generalize this sample to the greater population of Comp 110 students. These findings are valid; however, there aren't many implications to these findings.
